news_summary: Today's technology news highlights groundbreaking advancements in AI-driven space exploration, novel scientific measurements at the particle level, unexpected discoveries in genetics, and innovative contributions from early-career researchers driving disruptive science.
news_summary_fr: L'actualité technologique d'aujourd'hui met en avant des avancées révolutionnaires dans le domaine de l'exploration spatiale guidée par l'IA, des mesures scientifiques novatrices à l'échelle des particules, des découvertes inattendues en génétique, ainsi que les contributions innovantes de jeunes chercheurs à l'origine de percées scientifiques majeures.
news_summary_es: Las noticias tecnológicas de hoy destacan avances revolucionarios en la exploración espacial impulsada por la inteligencia artificial, novedosas mediciones científicas a nivel de partículas, descubrimientos inesperados en genética y contribuciones innovadoras de investigadores noveles que están impulsando avances científicos disruptivos.

articles.title: NASA’s Prithvi Becomes First AI Geospatial Foundation Model In Orbit
articles.summary: A team of researchers demonstrated NASA and IBM’s open-source Prithvi Geospatial artificial intelligence foundation model aboard two in-orbit platforms.
articles.content:
Florida as seen from the International Space Station. A NASA geospatial AI foundation model was deployed to a platform aboard the space station for the first time, unlocking new opportunities for Earth observation. NASA

Editor's Note: This article was updated May 7, 2025 to include a link to the preprint article for this research.

A team of researchers from Adelaide University and the SmartSat Cooperative Research Center in South Australia has successfully uploaded and demonstrated NASA and IBM’s open-source Prithvi Geospatial artificial intelligence (AI) foundation model aboard two in-orbit platforms, making it the first geospatial foundation model to be deployed in orbit. Trained on 13 years’ worth of data, Prithvi can facilitate a wide variety of Earth observation tasks.

By uploading a compressed version of Prithvi to the South Australian government’s Kanyini satellite and to the Thales Alenia Space IMAGIN-e (ISS Mounted Accessible Global Imaging Nod-e) payload aboard the International Space Station, the researchers tested the model’s flood and cloud detection performance across two different orbiting platforms and computing environments. The team shared their results in a preprint article.

Prithvi’s demo prediction of burn scars from the Gifford Fire, which occurred northwest of Los Angeles on August 17, 2025. When deployed aboard an Earth-observing satellite, foundation models can perform advanced analyses before the data even reaches the ground. NASA

The team chose Prithvi for their research because of its strong generalization across Earth observation tasks, and because of its availability as an open-source model.

“If Prithvi weren’t open source, I would have to train my own foundation model,” said Dr. Andrew Du, the project’s lead researcher, who is a postdoctoral researcher at Adelaide University and an AI engineer at the SmartSat Cooperative Research Center. “Having that model openly available saved a lot of time and effort.”

A foundation model is an AI model trained on an enormous amount of unlabeled data, which allows the model to begin detecting patterns in the data that humans wouldn’t notice on their own. The model can then be fine-tuned for specific applications using much smaller amounts of labeled data.

Flooding around Lake Norman in North Carolina caused by Hurricane Helene on October 7, 2024. The blue areas of the image are the Prithvi foundation model demo’s prediction of the extent of the flooding. NASA

“Prithvi is the first model of its kind to be deployed in orbit, and that demonstrates exactly why we make our AI models open source,” said Kevin Murphy, chief science data officer at NASA Headquarters in Washington, whose office led the collaboration that created Prithvi. “By sharing these tools with anyone who wants to use them, we accelerate scientific and technological development into the future.”

Developed by a team of data scientists from IBM and NASA’s IMPACT team within the Office of Data Science and Informatics at NASA’s Marshall Space Flight Center in Huntsville, Alabama, the Prithvi Geospatial model was trained on the Harmonized Landsat and Sentinel-2 dataset. This dataset compiles over a decade of global geospatial data from NASA’s Landsat and ESA (European Space Agency) Sentinel-2 satellites. Prithvi can be adapted for tasks such as mapping flood plains, monitoring disasters, and predicting crop yields.

By sharing these tools with anyone who wants to use them, we accelerate scientific and technological development into the future. Kevin Murphy NASA Chief Science Data Officer and Acting Chief Data Officer/Chief AI Officer

Earth-observing satellites collect enormous amounts of data about our planet. Processing and analyzing the data in orbit before the satellite sends it back to Earth can help researchers gain insights more quickly. However, active satellites often can’t accept large software updates because of bandwidth limits, so the AI models they carry for data analysis tend to be lightweight and highly specialized.

Researchers can use the flexibility of a foundation model to facilitate a wide range of Earth observation tasks in one software architecture. If they want the model to take on a new task once the satellite is in orbit, they only need to upload a small extra decoder package – using far less bandwidth than uploading a whole new model to the satellite.

On June 22, 2013, the Operational Land Imager (OLI) on Landsat 8 captured this false-color image of the East Peak fire burning in southern Colorado near Trinidad. Burned areas appear dark red, while actively burning areas look orange. Dark green areas are forests; light green areas are grasslands. Data from Landsat 8 were used to train the Prithvi foundation model, which can help detect burn scars. NASA Earth Observatory

Sending Prithvi to orbit is an early demonstration of how foundation models could transform Earth observation. In addition to data analysis, foundation models could eventually help scientists interact with the instruments collecting the data.

“A large language model is also a type of foundation model,” Du said. “In the future, this could allow operators to interact with satellites in natural language, asking questions about onboard data or system status and receiving responses in a conversational way.”

The NASA team behind Prithvi continues to work on open-source foundation models trained on NASA data. A heliophysics model, Surya, was released in 2025, and the team intends to create foundation models for planetary science, astrophysics, and biological and physical sciences as well.

The Prithvi Geospatial foundation model is funded by the Office of the Chief Science Data Officer within NASA's Science Mission Directorate at NASA Headquarters in Washington. The Office of the Chief Science Data Officer advances scientific discovery through innovative applications and partnerships in data science, advanced analytics, and artificial intelligence. To learn more about NASA’s AI foundation models and other AI tools for science, visit:

https://science.nasa.gov/artificial-intelligence-science

By Lauren Leese

Web Content Strategist for the Office of the Chief Science Data Officer
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant technological breakthrough where NASA and IBM's open-source Prithvi AI geospatial foundation model was successfully deployed aboard two in-orbit platforms, enabling advanced Earth observation tasks such as flood and burn scar detection. This innovation has broad implications for faster, more efficient environmental monitoring and disaster response, benefiting society at large. The story is focused, detailed, and highlights the open-source nature of the model, which accelerates scientific progress and has potential for wide-ranging applications.
articles.personality_title: NASA’s Prithvi AI model works in space to spot Earth changes faster
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – NASA and IBM created an artificial intelligence (AI) model named Prithvi that can analyze Earth images to detect events like floods and fires. This model uses over a decade of satellite data to learn patterns about Earth’s surface.

**What happened** – Researchers from Adelaide University and SmartSat Cooperative Research Center sent a smaller version of Prithvi to two satellites orbiting Earth, including one on the International Space Station. For the first time, this AI model ran in space, successfully spotting floods and burn scars from wildfires using data collected by the satellites.

**Impact** – Running the AI directly in space means satellites can analyze important changes on Earth faster, without waiting to send all data back to the ground. This helps scientists respond quicker to natural disasters like floods or fires. Also, because Prithvi is open source, anyone can use or improve it, speeding up future discoveries.

**What's next step** – Researchers plan to keep developing AI models like Prithvi for other science areas such as space weather and planetary studies. They also hope future satellites will let people talk to them in natural language, asking questions about the data they collect.

**One-sentence takeaway** – NASA’s Prithvi AI model is the first of its kind to work in orbit, enabling faster and smarter Earth monitoring from space.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le modèle IA Prithvi de la NASA fonctionne dans l’espace pour détecter plus rapidement les changements sur Terre
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La NASA et IBM ont créé un modèle d’intelligence artificielle (IA) appelé Prithvi, capable d’analyser des images de la Terre pour détecter des événements comme les inondations et les incendies. Ce modèle utilise plus d’une décennie de données satellites pour apprendre les caractéristiques de la surface terrestre.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs de l’Université d’Adélaïde et du SmartSat Cooperative Research Center ont envoyé une version réduite de Prithvi à deux satellites en orbite autour de la Terre, dont un à bord de la Station spatiale internationale. Pour la première fois, ce modèle IA a fonctionné dans l’espace, détectant avec succès des inondations et des cicatrices d’incendies grâce aux données collectées par les satellites.

**Impact** – Faire fonctionner l’IA directement dans l’espace permet aux satellites d’analyser plus rapidement les changements importants sur Terre, sans attendre que toutes les données soient renvoyées au sol. Cela aide les scientifiques à réagir plus vite aux catastrophes naturelles comme les inondations ou les incendies. De plus, comme Prithvi est open source, tout le monde peut l’utiliser ou l’améliorer, accélérant ainsi les découvertes futures.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs prévoient de continuer à développer des modèles IA comme Prithvi pour d’autres domaines scientifiques, comme la météo spatiale et l’étude des planètes. Ils espèrent aussi que les futurs satellites permettront de leur parler en langage naturel pour poser des questions sur les données collectées.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Le modèle IA Prithvi de la NASA est le premier du genre à fonctionner en orbite, permettant une surveillance plus rapide et plus intelligente de la Terre depuis l’espace.
articles.personality_title_es: El modelo IA Prithvi de la NASA funciona en el espacio para detectar cambios en la Tierra más rápido
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La NASA e IBM crearon un modelo de inteligencia artificial (IA) llamado Prithvi que puede analizar imágenes de la Tierra para detectar eventos como inundaciones e incendios. Este modelo usa más de una década de datos satelitales para aprender patrones sobre la superficie terrestre.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de la Universidad de Adelaida y el SmartSat Cooperative Research Center enviaron una versión reducida de Prithvi a dos satélites en órbita alrededor de la Tierra, incluido uno en la Estación Espacial Internacional. Por primera vez, este modelo de IA funcionó en el espacio, detectando con éxito inundaciones y cicatrices de incendios usando datos recogidos por los satélites.

**Impacto** – Ejecutar la IA directamente en el espacio significa que los satélites pueden analizar cambios importantes en la Tierra más rápido, sin esperar a enviar todos los datos a la Tierra. Esto ayuda a los científicos a responder más rápido a desastres naturales como inundaciones o incendios. Además, como Prithvi es de código abierto, cualquiera puede usarlo o mejorarlo, acelerando futuros descubrimientos.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores planean seguir desarrollando modelos de IA como Prithvi para otras áreas científicas, como el clima espacial y estudios planetarios. También esperan que futuros satélites permitan interactuar con ellos en lenguaje natural para hacer preguntas sobre los datos que recopilan.

**Resumen en una frase** – El modelo IA Prithvi de la NASA es el primero de su tipo en funcionar en órbita, permitiendo una vigilancia más rápida e inteligente de la Tierra desde el espacio.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of Earth from orbit showing the southeastern United States with glowing, translucent layers of digital data patterns overlaying the landscape, symbolizing AI analyzing flood zones and fire scars, while a sleek, stylized satellite and a softly illuminated space station float nearby, connected by gentle streams of light representing real-time geospatial AI processing.

articles.title: Pressure from individual particles measured for the first time
articles.summary: A device made using a tiny bead floating in a beam of light can measure extremely small pressures and could help find a mysterious kind of neutrino
articles.content:
A device made using a tiny bead floating in a beam of light can measure extremely small pressures and could help find a mysterious kind of neutrino

The ultra-sensitive pressure sensor features a 100-nanometre silica sphere held in place by laser light Thomas Penny/Yale Wright Laboratory

The pressure produced by a single particle can now be measured for the first time, thanks to a device that uses a tiny bead held in place by a laser. It is so sensitive that researchers hope that it could help find elusive new particles, such as those that could make up dark matter.

Pressure is caused by particles hitting an object and collectively exerting a force across its area. Researchers typically think of it as an average effect rather than zooming in on each particle, but when pressure is extremely low, such as in experiments conducted in near-perfect vacuum, tracking every particle is needed to properly account for its effects.

Yu-Han Tseng at Yale University and his colleagues have now built the first device capable of making such measurements. The central component is a tiny silica sphere, half the size of some viruses, held in place with a laser beam thanks to electromagnetic interactions between the two. Whenever a particle hits the sphere, it reflects light which the researchers can then detect.

To test this setup, the team placed the device into an ultra-high vacuum then systematically sent in particles of three different gases. They measured the device’s motion when hit by these particles, then calculated pressure from those measurements, compared it to mathematical predictions and found good agreement between the two – the device was doing exactly what they designed it for.

“You need to get everything right to get this measurement working,” says Tseng. “When we did everything carefully enough, the measurement turned out to be beautiful.”

Yu-Han Tseng, Thomas Penny and Cecily Lowe work on the pressure-sensing device

Subscriber-only newsletter Sign up to Lost in Space-Time Untangle mind-bending physics, maths and the weirdness of reality with our monthly, special-guest-written newsletter. Sign up to newsletter

Team member Clarke Hardy, also at Yale University, says that the new device could be used to establish a new definition for what counts as an extremely high vacuum where standard pressure sensors would simply read zero. “You could just count the number of collisions, and that would be good enough to give you an estimate of the pressure in these extreme high-vacuum regimes,” he says.

“Individual molecular collisions are rarely observed in real time. Traditionally, their effects are only seen on average, like how a fast-moving object appears blurred in a long-exposure photograph,” says Joseph Kelly at King’s College London.

Animesh Datta at the University of Warwick in the UK says that similar device design, including some that his own team has been developing, could be used in astronomy, for example helping us understand the low pressure spaces between stars better by detecting gas particles that reside there but may have been missed by other sensors.

But the team have another goal in mind – using the device to detect hypothetical so-called sterile neutrino particles, which could resolve decades-old anomalies in particle-physics experiments, explain why particles with incredibly tiny masses exist in our universe and even be a convincing candidate for what dark matter is made of.
articles.reasoning: The article describes a novel scientific device that can measure pressure from individual particles for the first time, representing a significant technological breakthrough with broad implications for physics, astronomy, and understanding dark matter. The device's ability to detect extremely low pressures and individual molecular collisions has potential wide-ranging impact in fundamental science and could lead to new discoveries about the universe.
articles.personality_title: New device measures pressure from single particles for the first time
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Pressure is usually measured as the combined force from many particles hitting a surface. But at extremely low pressures, like in near-empty spaces, scientists need to track each particle separately to understand how pressure works.

**What happened** – Researchers at Yale University built a tiny device using a 100-nanometre silica bead held in place by a laser beam. When particles hit this bead, it moves slightly, and the device measures that motion to calculate pressure from individual particles. The team tested it in a very strong vacuum with different gases and found the results matched their predictions.

**Impact** – This is the first time pressure from single particles has been measured directly. The device is so sensitive that it could help scientists study very empty spaces in labs or even between stars. It may also help find mysterious particles called sterile neutrinos, which could explain some big puzzles in physics and the nature of dark matter.

**What's next step** – The researchers plan to use the device to explore new physics, including searching for sterile neutrinos. They also hope it will set new standards for measuring ultra-low pressure in scientific experiments and improve our understanding of space environments.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists created a tiny laser-held bead device that can measure the pressure from single particles, opening new possibilities in physics and space research.
articles.personality_title_fr: Un nouvel appareil mesure la pression des particules individuelles pour la première fois
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La pression est généralement mesurée comme la force combinée de nombreuses particules frappant une surface. Mais à des pressions très faibles, comme dans des espaces presque vides, les scientifiques doivent suivre chaque particule séparément pour comprendre comment la pression fonctionne.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Des chercheurs de l'université de Yale ont construit un petit appareil utilisant une bille de silice de 100 nanomètres maintenue en place par un faisceau laser. Lorsque des particules frappent cette bille, elle bouge légèrement, et l'appareil mesure ce mouvement pour calculer la pression de chaque particule. L'équipe l'a testé dans un vide très poussé avec différents gaz et a trouvé que les résultats correspondaient à leurs prévisions.

**Impact** – C'est la première fois que la pression de particules individuelles est mesurée directement. L'appareil est tellement sensible qu'il pourrait aider les scientifiques à étudier des espaces très vides dans les laboratoires ou même entre les étoiles. Il pourrait aussi aider à découvrir des particules mystérieuses appelées neutrinos stériles, qui pourraient expliquer certains grands mystères en physique et la nature de la matière noire.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs prévoient d'utiliser l'appareil pour explorer de nouvelles physiques, notamment la recherche des neutrinos stériles. Ils espèrent aussi établir de nouvelles normes pour mesurer des pressions ultra-faibles dans les expériences scientifiques et mieux comprendre les environnements spatiaux.

**Phrase clé** – Des scientifiques ont créé un petit appareil avec une bille maintenue par laser qui peut mesurer la pression de particules individuelles, ouvrant de nouvelles possibilités en physique et en recherche spatiale.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevo dispositivo mide por primera vez la presión de partículas individuales
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La presión se mide normalmente como la fuerza combinada de muchas partículas que golpean una superficie. Pero a presiones muy bajas, como en espacios casi vacíos, los científicos necesitan seguir cada partícula por separado para entender cómo funciona la presión.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de la Universidad de Yale construyeron un pequeño dispositivo usando una esfera de sílice de 100 nanómetros sostenida en su lugar por un rayo láser. Cuando las partículas golpean esta esfera, se mueve un poco y el dispositivo mide ese movimiento para calcular la presión de partículas individuales. El equipo lo probó en un vacío muy fuerte con diferentes gases y encontró que los resultados coincidían con sus predicciones.

**Impacto** – Es la primera vez que se mide directamente la presión de partículas individuales. El dispositivo es tan sensible que podría ayudar a los científicos a estudiar espacios muy vacíos en laboratorios o incluso entre estrellas. También podría ayudar a encontrar partículas misteriosas llamadas neutrinos estériles, que podrían explicar grandes enigmas en la física y la naturaleza de la materia oscura.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores planean usar el dispositivo para explorar nueva física, incluyendo la búsqueda de neutrinos estériles. También esperan que establezca nuevos estándares para medir presiones ultra bajas en experimentos científicos y mejore nuestra comprensión de los ambientes espaciales.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Científicos crearon un pequeño dispositivo con una esfera sostenida por láser que puede medir la presión de partículas individuales, abriendo nuevas posibilidades en la física y la investigación espacial.
articles.image_prompt: A delicate, glowing 100-nanometre silica sphere suspended mid-air by a soft, radiant laser beam, gently vibrating as tiny, luminous particles collide with it one by one inside a tranquil, abstract vacuum chamber rendered with warm, muted earth tones and subtle light reflections.

articles.title: Scientists accidentally discover DNA that breaks the rules of life
articles.summary: A routine experiment with a new single-cell DNA sequencing method turned into a surprising scientific twist when researchers stumbled upon a bizarre genetic code in a microscopic pond organism. Instead of following the near-universal “rules” of life, this newly identified protist rewrites how genes signal their end. This unexpected discovery challenges long-held assumptions about how genetic translation works and hints that nature may be far more flexible—and mysterious—than scientists realized.
articles.content:
A test designed to push the limits of single cell DNA sequencing ended up revealing something far more surprising: a microscopic organism from a pond at Oxford University Parks appears to use the genetic code in a way scientists had not seen before.

Dr. Jamie McGowan, a postdoctoral scientist at the Earlham Institute, was studying the genome of a protist collected from freshwater. The goal was practical. Researchers wanted to test a DNA sequencing pipeline that could work with extremely small amounts of DNA, including DNA from a single cell.

Instead, the team found an unexpected genetic outlier. The organism, identified as Oligohymenophorea sp. PL0344, turned out to be a previously unknown species with a rare change in how it reads DNA instructions and builds proteins. The PLOS Genetics study reported that two codons normally associated with gene stopping signals had been reassigned to different amino acids, a combination the researchers described as previously unreported.

"It's sheer luck we chose this protist to test our sequencing pipeline, and it just shows what's out there, highlighting just how little we know about the genetics of protists."

A Tiny Organism With a Big Genetic Surprise

Protists are difficult to define neatly because they are so diverse. Many are microscopic, single celled organisms, including amoebas, algae, and diatoms. Others are much larger and multicellular, such as kelp, slime molds, and red algae.

"The definition of a protist is loose -- essentially it is any eukaryotic organism which is not an animal, plant, or fungus," said Dr. McGowan. "This is obviously very general, and that's because protists are an extremely variable group.

"Some are more closely related to animals, some more closely related to plants. There are hunters and prey, parasites and hosts, swimmers and sitters, and there are those with varied diets while others photosynthesize. Basically, we can make very few generalizations."

Oligohymenophorea sp. PL0344 belongs to a group called ciliates. These swimming protists can be seen under a microscope and are found in many watery environments. Ciliates have become especially interesting to geneticists because they are known hotspots for genetic code changes, including changes involving stop codons.

When Genetic Stop Signs Change Meaning

In most living things, three stop codons tell the cell where a gene ends: TAA, TAG, and TGA. These work like punctuation marks in the genetic instructions, signaling that protein construction should stop.

The genetic code is usually described as nearly universal because most organisms use the same basic rules. Variations do occur, but they are rare. In the small number of known genetic code variants, TAA and TAG usually change together and usually end up meaning the same thing. That pattern suggested the two codons were evolutionarily linked.

"In almost every other case we know of, TAA and TAG change in tandem," explained Dr. McGowan. "When they aren't stop codons, they each specify the same amino acid."

This organism did something different. In Oligohymenophorea sp. PL0344, only TGA appears to function as a stop codon. The other two signals have been repurposed. TAA specifies lysine, while TAG specifies glutamic acid. The researchers also found more TGA codons than expected, which may help compensate for the loss of the other two stop signals. The PLOS Genetics paper reported that the remaining UGA stop codon is enriched just after coding regions, suggesting it may help prevent harmful readthrough when translation continues too far.

"This is extremely unusual," Dr. McGowan said. "We're not aware of any other case where these stop codons are linked to two different amino acids. It breaks some of the rules we thought we knew about gene translation -- these two codons were thought to be coupled.

"Scientists attempt to engineer new genetic codes -- but they are also out there in nature. There are fascinating things we can find, if we look for them.

"Or, in this case, when we are not looking for them."

How Cells Read DNA Instructions

DNA can be thought of as a set of instructions, but the instructions must be copied and interpreted before they have an effect. First, a gene is transcribed into RNA. That RNA copy is then translated into amino acids, which are linked together to form proteins and other functional molecules.

Translation begins at the DNA start codon (ATG) and normally ends at a stop codon (normally TAA, TAG, or TGA). In this ciliate, that familiar ending system has been rearranged. The discovery shows that even one of biology's most conserved systems can be more flexible than expected.

The team's genome and transcriptome analysis also identified suppressor tRNA genes that match the reassigned codons, supporting the conclusion that the organism truly reads these former stop signals as amino acids. In the study, UAA was found to code for lysine and UAG for glutamic acid.

Later Work Shows Ciliates Are Genetic Rule Breakers

Follow up work has strengthened the idea that ciliates are unusually rich sources of genetic code surprises. In a 2024 PLOS Genetics study, researchers reported multiple independent reassignments of the UAG stop codon in phyllopharyngean ciliates. Some uncultivated ciliates from the TARA Oceans dataset appear to use UAG to encode leucine, while Hartmannula sinica and Trochilia petrani were found to use UAG to encode glutamine.

That later study also found that UAA remains the preferred stop codon in those phyllopharyngean ciliates, while UAG has repeatedly shifted into a protein coding role. The findings point to repeated changes in the genetic code across poorly studied microbial eukaryotes and reinforce the idea that ciliates are among the strongest exceptions to the standard genetic code.

Together, these discoveries suggest that the genetic code is not as fixed as it once seemed. For most organisms, the rules remain remarkably stable. But in overlooked microbial life, especially ciliates, evolution has repeatedly found ways to edit the instructions.

Funding and Publication

The original research was published in PLOS Genetics in 2023. It was funded by the Wellcome Trust as part of the Darwin Tree of Life Project and supported by the Earlham Institute's core funding from the Biotechnology and Biological Sciences Research Council (BBSRC), part of UKRI. The publication reported sequencing data and genome assembly resources deposited in public repositories.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery revealing unexpected flexibility in the genetic code of a microscopic organism, challenging long-held assumptions in biology. This breakthrough has broad implications for genetics and molecular biology, expanding our understanding of life’s fundamental mechanisms. The discovery is well-detailed, based on rigorous research, and published in a reputable scientific journal, indicating substantial substance and real-world impact on the scientific community and potentially biotechnology.
articles.personality_title: Scientists find tiny organism that breaks genetic code rules
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists usually believe that the genetic code, which tells cells how to build proteins, is almost the same for all living things. This code uses special signals called stop codons to tell cells where to stop making a protein.

**What happened** – While testing a new method to read DNA from a single cell, researchers at the Earlham Institute found a tiny organism in a pond near Oxford University that uses the genetic code differently. This organism, called Oligohymenophorea sp. PL0344, changes two usual stop codons into signals for making amino acids, breaking the normal rules.

**Impact** – This discovery is important because it shows that the genetic code can be more flexible than scientists thought. The organism uses TAA and TAG codons, normally stop signs, to make different amino acids instead. Only TGA still works as a stop codon. This is the first known case where two stop codons are reassigned to different amino acids, which changes how we understand gene translation.

**What's next step** – Researchers will study more ciliates and other tiny organisms to see if similar changes happen elsewhere. This could help scientists learn more about evolution and might even inspire new ways to engineer genetic codes for medicine or biotechnology.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A tiny pond organism rewrites genetic rules by turning stop signals into amino acid codes, revealing unexpected flexibility in life’s instructions.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques découvrent un organisme minuscule qui casse les règles du code génétique
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques pensent généralement que le code génétique, qui indique aux cellules comment fabriquer des protéines, est presque identique chez tous les êtres vivants. Ce code utilise des signaux spéciaux appelés codons stop pour dire aux cellules quand arrêter la fabrication d'une protéine.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – En testant une nouvelle méthode pour lire l’ADN d’une seule cellule, des chercheurs de l’Earlham Institute ont trouvé un petit organisme dans un étang près de l’Université d’Oxford qui utilise le code génétique différemment. Cet organisme, nommé Oligohymenophorea sp. PL0344, transforme deux codons stop habituels en signaux pour fabriquer des acides aminés, brisant ainsi les règles normales.

**Impact** – Cette découverte est importante car elle montre que le code génétique peut être plus flexible que ce que les scientifiques pensaient. L’organisme utilise les codons TAA et TAG, normalement des arrêts, pour créer des acides aminés différents. Seul le TGA reste un codon stop. C’est le premier cas connu où deux codons stop sont réaffectés à différents acides aminés, ce qui change notre compréhension de la traduction des gènes.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs vont étudier d’autres ciliés et petits organismes pour voir si des changements similaires existent ailleurs. Cela pourrait aider à mieux comprendre l’évolution et inspirer de nouvelles façons de modifier les codes génétiques pour la médecine ou la biotechnologie.

**Une phrase clé** – Un petit organisme d’étang réécrit les règles génétiques en transformant des signaux d’arrêt en codes d’acides aminés, révélant une flexibilité inattendue dans les instructions de la vie.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos descubren un organismo diminuto que rompe las reglas del código genético
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos suelen pensar que el código genético, que indica a las células cómo construir proteínas, es casi igual en todos los seres vivos. Este código usa señales especiales llamadas codones de parada para decir a las células cuándo detener la producción de una proteína.

**Qué pasó** – Al probar un nuevo método para leer el ADN de una sola célula, investigadores del Earlham Institute encontraron un organismo muy pequeño en un estanque cerca de la Universidad de Oxford que usa el código genético de forma diferente. Este organismo, llamado Oligohymenophorea sp. PL0344, cambia dos codones de parada normales en señales para crear aminoácidos, rompiendo las reglas habituales.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento es importante porque muestra que el código genético puede ser más flexible de lo que se pensaba. El organismo usa los codones TAA y TAG, que normalmente indican detenerse, para fabricar diferentes aminoácidos. Solo el TGA sigue funcionando como codón de parada. Es el primer caso conocido donde dos codones de parada se asignan a diferentes aminoácidos, cambiando cómo entendemos la traducción genética.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores estudiarán más ciliados y otros organismos pequeños para ver si estos cambios ocurren en otros lugares. Esto podría ayudar a entender mejor la evolución y podría inspirar nuevas formas de modificar códigos genéticos para la medicina o biotecnología.

**Una frase clave** – Un organismo diminuto de un estanque reescribe las reglas genéticas al convertir señales de parada en códigos de aminoácidos, revelando una flexibilidad inesperada en las instrucciones de la vida.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a glowing, translucent single-celled ciliate organism swimming in a softly lit freshwater pond environment, its internal structure subtly visualized as intertwined strands of DNA with colorful, glowing codon sequences—some codons glowing differently to symbolize the unusual genetic code reassignments—surrounded by delicate bubbles and gentle ripples that evoke discovery and the mystery of microscopic life.

articles.title: Early-career researchers do more ‘disruptive’ science than veterans
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 07 May 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01466-zAnalysis of papers from millions of scientists shows that older researchers tend to stick with ideas from their past.
articles.content:
Experienced researchers are more likely to hold onto ideas from the past than those at the beginning of their careers, an analysis finds.Credit: RapidEye/Getty

Experienced researchers are less likely to produce ‘disruptive’ science than are those just starting their careers, finds an analysis of the scientific papers published by 12.5 million researchers over 60 years. The authors discovered that older researchers are better at connecting existing ideas to produce new knowledge than are younger researchers. But those with more experience are worse at achieving massive breakthroughs that overhaul, or disrupt, entire fields of research — as happened with innovations such as the discovery of the structure of DNA.

Are groundbreaking science discoveries becoming harder to find?

The analysis, which was published today in Science1, also concludes that, as their careers progress, scientists are more likely to cite older papers than newer ones. This phenomenon, which the authors call the nostalgia effect, can hold back scientific innovation, they say, because scientists get hung up on ideas from the past and are not as receptive to new developments.

The finding isn’t surprising — it aligns with previous studies documenting a decades-long global decline in disruptive science as the scientific workforce ages, says Russell Funk, a sociologist at the University of Minnesota in Minneapolis who was not involved in the latest analysis. But it does identify a potential mechanism for the trend, he says. “Scientists become less disruptive as they age, and the scientific workforce is getting older, so the entire system is shifting toward a composition that favours consolidation [of existing ideas] over disruption,” he says.

Scientific nostalgia

To measure how disruptive a scientific paper is, the research team examined how later studies cite it. If those studies cite the paper without also citing its references — which represent previous work in a field — this suggests that the idea or discovery reported in the paper rendered older work obsolete. This is a sign of disruption, the team says.

When the authors studied a data set including the work of 12.5 million researchers who had published at least three papers between 1960 and 2020, they found that, across all fields, the probability of a researcher producing a paper among the top 10% most disruptive works decreased with their academic age — defined as the number of years since their first publication (see ‘Disruption decline’).

Source: Ref. 1

The researchers also found that the paper a scientist cites most often during their career is typically published about two years before they publish their own first paper. This suggests that “as you age, you’re still holding on to those formative years”, says Raiyan Abdul Baten, a computational social scientist at the University of South Florida in Tampa. “That hurts you in terms of moving forward and producing disruptive new work.”

Lingfei Wu, an information scientist at the University of Pittsburgh in Pennsylvania and a co-author of the analysis, says that it’s not surprising that researchers are deeply affected by ideas circulating early in their careers. What is unexpected, he adds, is how long they are influenced by those ideas.

The influence of citations

Choices made by senior researchers can have consequences for the scientific community. For example, Wu and his colleagues looked at the papers produced by more than 190,000 research teams when they changed their corresponding author between publications. The researchers found that when a paper’s corresponding author was younger, the work tended to cite fresher references than when its corresponding author was older. This has implications for what science is being pointed to as noteworthy, the team says.
articles.reasoning: The article presents a significant and well-supported analysis revealing that early-career researchers tend to produce more disruptive, groundbreaking science compared to veteran scientists, highlighting a mechanism that influences scientific innovation. This insight has broad implications for the scientific community and society by identifying factors that could help foster more innovation and breakthroughs in research.
articles.personality_title: Young scientists lead in groundbreaking research, study finds
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists often build on past ideas, but how much they challenge old knowledge can change with experience. A new study looked at how researchers of different ages contribute to science over time.

**What happened** – Researchers analyzed the published papers of 12.5 million scientists from 1960 to 2020. They found that younger scientists are more likely to create 'disruptive' work—research that changes or replaces old ideas—while older scientists tend to connect existing ideas without making big breakthroughs. They also noticed that scientists usually keep citing papers from their early career years, a habit called the "nostalgia effect."

**Impact** – This is important because it shows that as scientists get older, they rely more on past work and less on new ideas, which can slow down major discoveries. The study helps explain why major scientific breakthroughs may be less common as the research community ages. It also highlights how younger scientists bring fresh perspectives that can push science forward.

**What's next step** – Understanding this pattern could encourage universities and research groups to support younger scientists more and find ways to help experienced researchers stay open to new ideas. This might lead to more innovation and faster progress in science.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Younger scientists tend to produce more groundbreaking research, while older scientists focus on building from existing knowledge, shaping how science evolves over time.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les jeunes chercheurs en tête des découvertes majeures, révèle une étude
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques s'appuient souvent sur des idées anciennes, mais leur volonté de remettre en cause ces idées peut évoluer avec l'expérience. Une nouvelle étude a examiné comment les chercheurs de différents âges contribuent à la science au fil du temps.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Des chercheurs ont analysé les articles publiés par 12,5 millions de scientifiques entre 1960 et 2020. Ils ont découvert que les jeunes chercheurs ont plus tendance à produire des travaux « disruptifs » — des recherches qui changent ou remplacent des idées anciennes — tandis que les chercheurs plus âgés relient surtout des idées existantes sans faire de grandes découvertes. Ils ont aussi remarqué que les scientifiques continuent souvent de citer des articles de leurs premières années de carrière, un phénomène appelé « effet nostalgie ».

**Impact** – Cela est important car cela montre qu'avec l'âge, les chercheurs s'appuient davantage sur le passé et moins sur les nouveautés, ce qui peut ralentir les grandes découvertes. L'étude aide à comprendre pourquoi les avancées majeures sont peut-être moins fréquentes alors que la communauté scientifique vieillit. Elle souligne aussi que les jeunes chercheurs apportent des idées nouvelles qui font avancer la science.

**Prochaine étape** – Comprendre ce phénomène pourrait encourager les universités et les équipes de recherche à soutenir davantage les jeunes chercheurs et à aider les chercheurs expérimentés à rester ouverts aux nouvelles idées. Cela pourrait favoriser plus d'innovations et un progrès scientifique plus rapide.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Les jeunes chercheurs produisent plus souvent des découvertes majeures, tandis que les chercheurs plus âgés développent surtout les connaissances existantes, influençant ainsi l'évolution de la science.
articles.personality_title_es: Los jóvenes científicos lideran la investigación innovadora, según estudio
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos suelen basarse en ideas anteriores, pero su disposición a cuestionar esas ideas puede cambiar con la experiencia. Un nuevo estudio analizó cómo investigadores de distintas edades contribuyen a la ciencia a lo largo del tiempo.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores analizaron los artículos publicados por 12,5 millones de científicos entre 1960 y 2020. Descubrieron que los científicos jóvenes tienen más probabilidades de crear trabajos "disruptivos", investigaciones que cambian o reemplazan ideas antiguas, mientras que los científicos mayores tienden a conectar ideas existentes sin hacer grandes avances. También notaron que los científicos suelen citar artículos de sus primeros años de carrera, un hábito llamado "efecto nostalgia".

**Impacto** – Esto es importante porque muestra que a medida que los científicos envejecen, usan más el trabajo pasado y menos ideas nuevas, lo que puede ralentizar los grandes descubrimientos. El estudio ayuda a entender por qué los avances científicos importantes pueden ser menos comunes a medida que la comunidad científica envejece. También destaca cómo los científicos jóvenes aportan nuevas perspectivas que impulsan la ciencia.

**Próximo paso** – Comprender este patrón podría motivar a universidades y grupos de investigación a apoyar más a los científicos jóvenes y encontrar formas de ayudar a los investigadores experimentados a mantenerse abiertos a nuevas ideas. Esto podría generar más innovación y un progreso científico más rápido.

**Resumen en una frase** – Los científicos jóvenes tienden a producir investigaciones más innovadoras, mientras que los científicos mayores se enfocan en construir sobre el conocimiento existente, moldeando así la evolución de la ciencia.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a young sapling breaking through a bed of aged, intertwined roots and old books, symbolizing early-career researchers growing disruptive new ideas amidst the established knowledge of experienced scientists, using soft natural earth tones and gentle light to evoke optimism and innovation.
